system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 11: Jazz Standard Study #10: Stella by Starlight
Pages 168-187 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

So here we are; the last jazz standard study of this book. I’ve saved
the most difficult for last, but also an enjoyable one to explore. Stella
By Starlight, curiously enough, is a song that a lot of beginners learn
early on. This was indeed true for me. | find that interesting because
Stella is among one of the more difficult jazz standards to understand
harmonically.

However, the previous jazz standards studies will help quite a bit in un-
derstanding this song. These songs have been ordered with purpose.

As is the case with many jazz standards, Stella has a beautiful mel-
ody. It’s a song | would especially suggest learning the lyrics to or at
least listening to lots of versions by vocalists.

Stella by Starlight was written by Victor Young for the 1944 film “The Undecid-
ed.” Ned Washington later wrote lyrics to it. At different times it was adopted
by influential jazz musicians such as Frank Sinatra, Charlie Parker, Stan Getz
and Miles Davis, making it a standard song in jazz repertoire. This song is most
commonly played in the key of concert Bb major.

This is important: listen to the original version. I’ve included a link in
the suggested listening. It’s always good to listen to original record-
ings, but in this particular case, it’s even more important.

Why? The chord changes that jazz musicians play are different.
They have the same essence, but because of the influence of mu-
sicians like Miles Davis and the advent of the Real Book, the chord
changes have evolved into more traditional jazz harmony. If you were
to learn the chord changes from the original version, you wouldn't be
playing the same stuff as other jazz musicians will on gigs and jam
sessions. That’s my disclaimer. In the chord chart, I’ve included the
first chord used in the original version, just to give you some context.


>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Original version from “The Uninvited” 1944

Frank Sinatra from “The Columbia Years”
(Compilation) 1943-1952

Miles Davis from “1958 Miles” 1958

Charlie Parker from “Charlie Parker With Strings”

Recorded: 1947-1952

Ella Fitzgerald from “The Voice of Jazz” 1962

There is so much to unpack in this tune, so let’s get started. When
preparing for writing this chapter | spent a great deal of time debating
how best to color code this song. The primary goal of providing col-
or-coded sections for you is to make these jazz standards easier to
understand. However, Stella presented quite the challenge.

It’s quite tricky to boil this song down to key centers like Have You Met
Miss Jones and All the Things You Are. My strategy here was to re-
late all of the roman numerals back to the parent key while color-cod-
ing what | deemed important to think about as targeting different key
centers.

Blue = Bb major (parent key)

Green = D minor (iii chord of parent)
Orange = Eb major (IV chord of parent)
‘Purple: = F major (V major or parent)
‘Yellow = G major (VI7b13 of parent)
Red = C minor (ii of parent)

Let’s take a look at the first section and see what is going on.

Ewin? A’ Con? F7

ii-V of iii (unresolved) ii-V of Tonic (unresolved)

Fun? BY E?was? Ar?

*  W-IFIVT of IV -------------------- "2-222-222-2222 2-2022222222--e
(backdoor dominant to I)

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK [MyJ



The first thing to note is that we have a series of unresolved ii-V chord
progressions within the first 8 bars. We have seen these before. This
just means the progressions do not resolve to the | chord but move
somewhere else. The key center is being eluded to, yet never comes
home.

Again, | have related the roman numerals back to the parent key, so
the first two bars are an unresolved ii-V of iii.

Bars 2-4 are an unresolved ii-V to the tonic or parent key. If you were
to look at the chords individually in these first 4 bars, it would be hard
to see the connection. But by recognizing that these are unresolved
progressions targeting the iii chord and the | chord, we can make some
sense of their harmonic foundations.

Bars 5-7 are a very clear ii-V-| to the IV chord. This is a typical move-
ment we have seen in our other jazz standard studies.

But there are two ways to perceive the Ab7 in bar 8. You can think of
it as a V7 of the IV chord (fairly common), or you can recognize it as
a backdoor dominant moving into the | chord in bar 10. Understanding
backdoor dominants is helpful for this song because it comes up sev-
eral more times.

>> WHAT'S A BACKDOOR DOMINANT?

A backdoor dominant is a dominant 7" chord that substitutes the V7
chord for a bVII7 chord approaching the | chord by a whole step. This
works because the bVII7 has a lot of notes in common with an altered
V7 chord.

Example: Bb7-Cmaj/

In just a little bit we will be discussing a backdoor ii-V, so hold that
thought for a second. Let’s take a look at the next section.

177

The first time we see the | chord is in bar 9 but quickly moves to a ii-V
of iii.

It’s in bar 12 we meet the backdoor ii-V. | think it’s easiest to think of
this progression as a backdoor progression. Otherwise, the chord it re-
solves to would be completely unrelated to both the parent key center
or the key center the ii-V of bVII is targeting. You'll want to be familiar

with backdoor ii-V’s as you will see them in other jazz standards such
as “Lady Bird” by Tadd Dameron.

The ii chord is being added in front of the backdoor dominant. There-
fore, the Bbmin7-Eb7 is really acting as a backdoor ii-V into the Fmaj7.
| know this may seem confusing, and it is. But if you can simplify this
progression by thinking of it as a backdoor progression, it gets easier
to compartmentalize these chords.

In bar 13 I’ve labeled the Fmaj7 as a Vmaj7 chord. Obviously, a V chord
is never a major chord. However, | think it is still helpful to relate it to
the parent key. In reality, we are very temporarily landing in the key of
F major.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK [Mygj



Bar 14 has a quick unresolved ii-V of iii and then bar 15 starts a ii-V of
vi. But of course, it can’t just be that easy. Instead of this ii-V resolving
to the minor vi, it resolves to a VI7b18.

Now, it’s important to look at this chord in context. Where is it com-
ing from and where is it going? If you look at where it’s coming from,
it seems most natural to think of it as an altered dominant VI chord.
But if you look at where it’s going, it almost seems to be acting as a
V chord of the Cmin7 (if you consider the Cmin7 ai chord). Ultimately,
| decided to think of the Cmin7 in bar 19 as a ii chord, related to the
parent key center. There perhaps is no right or wrong way to think of it.

Let’s take a look at the next section.

There’s that backdoor dominant again! This time it’s an Ab7 with a
#11, because the #11 is in the melody. Again, it’s approaching the Bb-
maj/7 a whole step from below.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK [yg



Bar 25 marks the last section of the song. The first two bars are the
same as the beginning of the tune, but this time we continue on with a
cycle of 4ths. This cycle of 4ths is different than we have seen before
in that they are a series of minor ii-V’s. In bars 26 and 27 it’s an unre-
solved ii-V of ii.

The last four bars demonstrate what | call a hybrid ii-V-I.

>> WHAT'S A HYBRID II-V-I?

A hybrid ii-V-I is when a minor ii-V-i is mixed with a major ii-V-l. The ii
and V are borrowed from the minor key, but they resolve to the major |.

Example: Dmin7(b5)-G7(b9)-Cmaj7

Hybrid ii-V-l’s come up in lots of other jazz standards such as the Cole
Porter songs “What is This Thing Called Love” and “I Love You.” You
definitely want to be familiar with them. This compositional technique
is used to add a sense of surprise. When you hear the minor ii-V being
played, it gives you the sense that it will resolve to a minor chord, but
then unexpectedly, it resolves to a major chord.

If your mind is completely boggled right now by Stella by Starlight,
don’t worry. This is a tricky one to understand, and | would encourage
you to take your time looking through the chords analysis and my ex-
planations.

What | especially want you to take away from this study is the concept
of backdoor dominants with ii-V’s and hybrid ii-V’s. These will come up
time and time again throughout your studies of jazz standards.


IMPROV LESSON
Using Nete Maps as w Toot

We’ve covered some jazz standards in this book that are difficult to
improvise over, but | think Stella wins that award by far. Throughout
our studies, we’ve used the idea of mapping out notes through chord
tones and scales, but in this improv lesson, | want to take it a little bit
further.

When we come across songs like Stella, it can be beneficial to take a
step back and map out the notes we have available to us. While scales
are not musical by themselves, they can help us conceptualize which
notes we can choose from. From there, we can start to build melodies
and make sense of how to connect the harmony in our lines.

The first thing | want to demonstrate is a Note Choice Map of Stella
by Starlight. In this note map, | have spelled out different modes and
scales that accurately represent the note choices in each chord. Now,
| have already shared formulas for the modes in our So What study, so
be sure to refresh your memory if you need to.

The Altered Scale and the Lydian Dominant are the ones you haven't
seen before in this book, but | do have them clearly spelled out. For a
more thorough, encyclopedic resource for scales, check out my other
book | have mentioned several times, Zero to Improv.

Take a look and study the next few pages.

I’ve mentioned him in this book once before, but | want to give him
credit again. Josiah Boornazian, an excellent jazz musician and ed-
ucator, first inspired me to create a note map like this over Stella. |
would suggest you also check out his note map of Stella, because he
makes even more colorful scale choices than | do here.

So now what? Where do you go from here? Playing scales like this over
chords is hardly musical, so we don’t want to leave it like this. We need
to apply this information and make it musical.

One exercise | like to do to keep things simple and help me start to
improvise is create quarter note solos. This means | stick to a quarter
note rhythm in all of my note choices. | take out the rhythmic compo-
nent from my improvisation and just focus on navigating the changes.

On the next page, I’ve composed a quarter note solo using the scales
from the note map. | have not wandered outside of those notes so
that | can work on using these scales to create actual melodies.

Take a look and play this solo.

To help make it clear which scales | am drawing my note choices from,
| have labeled them underneath the bars. Feel free to refer back to the
note map if you need help visualizing these notes.

My challenge to you is to create your own quarter note solo. Use the
note choice map | provided and work out your own. Even see if you can
improvise one on the fly, but composing is just fine.

| hope you have realized from all of these improv lessons that these
are merely different angles to approach improvisation. There are many
variations of things you can do to help you conceptualize improvisation
and build up a repertoire of jazz language. The more you work on exer-
cises like these, the more the mystery of jazz improvisation will fade.

Stella by Starlight is not an easy song. But it’s an incredible composi-
tion worth many years of study. Study the progressions to help make
sense of it, and use note mapping to help you fill in the blanks.
